message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 10 (all tests passing)
Recent Green phase: No change — negative coordinates work natively because the implementation only uses Chebyshev distance and equality.

This is the final test in the suite. Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
I'll run the metric-driven refactoring workflow on the Game of Life implementation.

## Step 0: Pre-Measurement (ESLint - Smells + Cognitive Complexity)

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>&1 | head -200
input.description: Run ESLint with JSON output to baseline

content.text:
**PRE Measurement — ESLint Smells**:
- ESLint smells: 0 (clean)
- Cognitive complexity warnings: 0

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

Let me evaluate each function name based on the full test suite (10 passing tests covering all Game of Life rules + negative coordinates):

| Function | Purpose | Name Fit |
|----------|---------|----------|
| `isNeighbor(a, b)` | True if Chebyshev distance is 1 | Good — reveals intent |
| `countLiveNeighbors(cell, cells)` | Counts neighbors of cell in live set | Good |
| `isAlive(cell, cells)` | True if cell in live set | Good |
| `cellWithNeighbors([x,y])` | Returns cell + 8 neighbors (Moore neighborhood) | Slightly misleading — includes the cell itself (offset [0,0]). Actually correct since we want the cell as a candidate too. The 3×3 block. |
| `cellKey([x,y])` | Stable string key for deduplication | Good |
| `candidateCells(cells)` | Unique union of all cells with their neighborhoods | Good |
| `livesNextGeneration(cell, cells)` | Applies B3/S23 rule | Good |
| `nextGeneration(cells)` | Public — computes next state | Good |

One observation: `cellWithNeighbors` returns the cell itself plus 8 neighbors (the [0,0] offset is included). The name is technically accurate ("cell with neighbors" = cell + neighbors), but `NEIGHBORHOOD_OFFSETS` contains [0,0] which is not actually an offset to a *neighbor*. Naming-wise, calling it `MOORE_NEIGHBORHOOD_OFFSETS` (including the center) or splitting cleanly would be clearer. Let me hold this thought and check the metrics first.

## Step 2: Pre-Measurement (APP Mass)

Let me compute APP mass for each function:

**`isNeighbor`** (lines 7-11):
- Constants: 1 (literal `1`)
- Bindings: 6 (a, b, ax, ay, bx, by)
- Invocations: 4 (Math.max, Math.abs, Math.abs, `-` twice... counting `-` as ops: 2 subtractions + 2 abs + 1 max = 5; `===` is comparison, +1)
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0
- Counting carefully: invocations = Math.max(1) + Math.abs(2) + 2 subtractions + 1 equality = 6
- Mass: 1·1 + 6·1 + 6·2 + 0 + 0 + 0 = **19**

**`countLiveNeighbors`** (lines 13-15):
- Constants: 0
- Bindings: 3 (cell, cells, other)
- Invocations: 2 (filter, isNeighbor) + .length access ≈ 2
- Loops: 1 (filter)
- Mass: 0 + 3 + 4 + 0 + 5 + 0 = **12**

**`isAlive`** (lines 17-20):
- Constants: 0
- Bindings: 4 (cell, cells, cx, cy, x, y) → 6
- Invocations: 1 (some) + 2 (`===` ×2) + 1 (`&&`) = 4
- Loops: 1 (some)
- Mass: 0 + 6 + 8 + 0 + 5 + 0 = **19**

**`cellWithNeighbors`** (lines 28-30):
- Constants: 0
- Bindings: 4 (x, y, dx, dy)
- Invocations: 1 (map) + 2 (`+` ×2) = 3
- Loops: 1 (map)
- Mass: 0 + 4 + 6 + 0 + 5 + 0 = **15**

**`cellKey`** (lines 32-34):
- Bindings: 2 (x, y)
- Invocations: 1 (template literal)
- Mass: 0 + 2 + 2 = **4**

**`candidateCells`** (lines 36-39):
- Bindings: 3 (cells, all, cell)
- Invocations: 5 (flatMap, cellWithNeighbors, Map ctor, map, cellKey, values, spread) ≈ 6
- Loops: 2 (flatMap, map)
- Mass: 0 + 3 + 12 + 0 + 10 + 0 = **25**

**`livesNextGeneration`** (lines 41-47):
- Constants: 0 (only named constants used)
- Bindings: 3 (cell, cells, count) + reference to constants (NEIGHBORS_TO_REPRODUCE, MIN_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE) = 5
- Invocations: countLiveNeighbors + isAlive + `===` ×2 + `&&` + `||` = 6
- Mass: 0 + 5 + 12 + 0 + 0 + 0 = **17**

**`nextGeneration`** (lines 49-51):
- Bindings: 2 (cells, cell)
- Invocations: 3 (candidateCells, filter, livesNextGeneration)
- Loops: 1 (filter)
- Mass: 0 + 2 + 6 + 0 + 5 + 0 = **13**

**Total Mass across all functions: 124**

## Step 3: Pre-Measurement (McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity)

| Function | McCabe Calculation | Score |
|----------|-------------------|-------|
| `isNeighbor` | Start 1, no branches | **1** |
| `countLiveNeighbors` | Start 1, no branches (filter is iteration, not branching at function level) | **1** |
| `isAlive` | Start 1, `&&` adds 1 | **2** |
| `cellWithNeighbors` | Start 1 | **1** |
| `cellKey` | Start 1 | **1** |
| `candidateCells` | Start 1 | **1** |
| `livesNextGeneration` | Start 1, `\|\|` +1, `&&` +1 | **3** |
| `nextGeneration` | Start 1 | **1** |

**Worst McCabe**: `livesNextGeneration` at 3 — and that's already very low. All others are 1 or 2.

## Refactoring Target Analysis

Looking at all measurements:
- ESLint: 0 smells, 0 cognitive complexity warnings
- McCabe: max 3 (livesNextGeneration)
- APP mass: highest is `candidateCells` at 25

The candidate cells flow uses a Map for deduplication, which is fairly complex (25 mass, 2 loops). Could it be simpler?

Looking at `candidateCells`:
```typescript
function candidateCells(cells: Cell[]): Cell[] {
  const all = cells.flatMap(cellWithNeighbors);
  return [...new Map(all.map((cell) => [cellKey(cell), cell])).values()];
}
```

This has a temporary binding `all` and two iteration steps. We could simplify by building the Map directly with reduce, but that's not clearer. Or we could merge into a single chain. Let me think about whether the intermediate `all` binding helps readability — actually yes, the two-step structure separates "gather candidates" from "deduplicate". Inlining might hurt clarity.

Looking at `livesNextGeneration` — the worst McCabe at 3. Can it be lower? The rule has two distinct cases (birth at 3, survive at 2 with already-alive). The structure mirrors Conway's rules directly. Reducing McCabe here would obscure the rules.

Let me look at one possible improvement: `NEIGHBORHOOD_OFFSETS` contains `[0, 0]` (the cell itself). The name "NEIGHBORHOOD" already implies including the center (the Moore neighborhood including self), but `cellWithNeighbors` reads slightly off because [0,0] isn't a neighbor offset. The current naming hides this. Two options:

**Option A**: Split into true neighbor offsets (8) and treat the cell separately in `cellWithNeighbors`. This adds complexity without benefit.

**Option B**: Rename to make the intent clearer — `MOORE_NEIGHBORHOOD_OFFSETS` (well-known term for 3×3 including center).

Let me also notice: in `livesNextGeneration`, the constant `MAX_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` is declared but unused! Let me verify.

`MAX_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` is declared but never used. It's also misleading because actually for survival you need 2-3 neighbors, and the constant says "MAX" is 3 — but the implementation uses `count === 3` (which it interprets as reproduction via `NEIGHBORS_TO_REPRODUCE`). The logic exploits the fact that birth and survival-with-3 produce the same outcome (live cell with 3 neighbors), so it's not represented as the canonical "B3/S23" rules. 

The current logic is:
- `count === 3` (treat as reproduction; covers both birth at 3 and survive at 3 since alive-or-dead doesn't matter)
- `count === 2 && isAlive` (survive at 2)

This is mathematically correct but the constant `MIN_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE = 2` is also a bit awkward — it's used as the exact survive count. Let me think about this as a refactoring opportunity.

**Refactoring Plan**: 
1. Remove the unused `MAX_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` constant (dead code — Rule 4 fewest elements)
2. Rename `MIN_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` → `NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE_IF_ALIVE` for clearer intent
3. Rename `NEIGHBORHOOD_OFFSETS` to clarify it includes the cell itself

Actually let me be disciplined and do ONE thing at a time. The clearest win is **removing unused dead code** (Rule 4) — this is a no-risk improvement.

## Step 4: Apply ONE Refactoring

Refactoring: Remove unused `MAX_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` constant and rename `MIN_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` to better reflect its use (it's the exact count when alive, not a min).

Wait — let me apply truly ONE change. The single highest-value refactoring: remove the unused constant.

content.text:
All 10 tests still passing. The reminder about TaskCreate is not relevant — this is a focused refactoring run with a defined workflow, not a multi-step tracked task.

## Step 5: Post-Measurement (ESLint)

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>&1 | head -50

content.text:
**POST ESLint**: 0 smells, 0 warnings (no regression).

## Step 6: Post-Measurement (APP + McCabe)

`livesNextGeneration` after change:
```typescript
function livesNextGeneration(cell: Cell, cells: Cell[]): boolean {
  const count = countLiveNeighbors(cell, cells);
  return (
    count === NEIGHBORS_TO_REPRODUCE ||
    (count === NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE && isAlive(cell, cells))
  );
}
```

- **APP mass**: unchanged (rename only) = **17**
- **McCabe**: unchanged = **3**

Top-level module:
- Constants declared: 3 → 2 (removed `MAX_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE`)
- That's a module-level reduction of one binding (Mass -1)

## Refactoring Delta Summary

**Refactoring Applied**: Removed unused constant `MAX_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE`. Renamed `MIN_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` → `NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` (the value `2` is the exact survive count, not a minimum — "MIN" implied an inequality the code doesn't use).

**Pre/Post Deltas**:
- ESLint smells: 0 → 0 (no regression)
- Cognitive complexity: 0 → 0 (no warnings before or after)
- APP mass (module-level constants): 3 bindings → 2 bindings (−1)
- APP mass (`livesNextGeneration`): 17 → 17 (rename only)
- McCabe (`livesNextGeneration`): 3 → 3 (no branch change)

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) Win**:
- `MAX_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE = 3` was both dead code AND misleading — implied "up to 3" inequality the code never used. Removing eliminates that misdirection.
- `NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` is more accurate than `MIN_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` — there is no inequality, the survival rule is exactly `count === 2 && isAlive`.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) Win**: Dead constant removed.

**Tests**: 10/10 passing.

## Why Other Candidates Were Not Refactored

- `candidateCells` has the highest APP mass (25) but the two-step structure (gather → dedupe) reveals intent better than a single chained line. Inlining `all` would hurt Rule 2.
- `livesNextGeneration` has the highest McCabe (3) but the structure directly mirrors Conway's B3/S23 rules. Collapsing the disjunction would obscure the rules.
- `cellWithNeighbors` / `NEIGHBORHOOD_OFFSETS` naming is mildly imperfect ([0,0] is in the offset list and isn't a "neighbor"), but the Moore neighborhood with center is a standard concept and the naming is acceptable. No high-confidence rename available.

## Step 7: Report

Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Removed dead constant `MAX_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE`; renamed `MIN_NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` → `NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE` for accurate intent
- **Smell delta**: 0 → 0
- **Cognitive delta** (livesNextGeneration): 0 → 0
- **APP mass delta** (module bindings): 3 → 2; (livesNextGeneration): 17 → 17
- **McCabe delta** (livesNextGeneration): 3 → 3
- **Tests**: 10/10 passing

File changed: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_16-34-03_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`
